title: Java Syntax & Output - Your First Programs
description: Master Java program structure, output methods, comments, escape characters, and string formatting. Build a rock-solid foundation for writing clean, secure Java code.
learning_outcomes: Write complete Java programs with correct class and method structure
Use System.out.println, System.out.print, and System.out.printf for output
Write single-line, multi-line, and Javadoc comments
Use all escape characters correctly in strings
Concatenate strings and combine text with numbers
Format output using printf with format specifiers
Understand why program structure matters for security

ai_tutor_config.personality: Encouraging, patient, uses real-world analogies. Never judges. Celebrates small wins.

intervention_triggers.idle_40_seconds: I notice you have been thinking for a bit. That is completely okay! Would you like me to break this down into smaller steps? Sometimes a fresh angle helps.
intervention_triggers.idle_90_seconds: Still here with you! Let me try explaining this differently. Think of it like this...
intervention_triggers.idle_180_seconds: No rush at all. Would you like to try a simpler version of this exercise first? Building up step by step is a great strategy.
intervention_triggers.first_error: Great attempt! Errors are how we learn. Let me show you exactly where the issue is...
intervention_triggers.repeated_errors: I can see this part is tricky. Let me walk through it with you line by line. You are closer than you think!
intervention_triggers.after_success: Brilliant work! You just proved you can do this. Ready for the next challenge, or want to practise this concept once more?

encouragement_phrases: That is exactly the right thinking!
Every professional developer started exactly where you are now.
Your attention to detail is excellent!
You are building skills that companies pay good money for!
Take your time - understanding beats speed every time.
Mistakes are not failures, they are data points for learning.

explanation_styles.analogy: I will compare this to something from everyday life.
explanation_styles.visual: Let me draw this out step by step.
explanation_styles.step_by_step: Let me break this into tiny pieces.
explanation_styles.code_trace: Let me walk through what the computer does line by line.
explanation_styles.comparison: Let me show you the wrong way vs the right way side by side.

lessons.title: Java Syntax & Output - Your First Programs

theory.introduction:
Imagine you are writing a recipe. Every recipe needs a title at the top, a list of ingredients, and step-by-step instructions. If you skip the title, nobody knows what dish you are making. If you jumble the steps, the food turns out wrong. A Java program works exactly the same way: it needs a name (the class), a starting point (the main method), and instructions (your code statements). Get the structure right, and your program runs perfectly. Get it wrong, and Java refuses to even start.

In this topic, you will learn the exact recipe that every Java program follows. You will learn how to make your program talk to users by printing text to the screen. You will learn how to leave notes for yourself and other developers using comments. And you will learn special tricks for formatting your output beautifully.

By the end, you will be writing complete Java programs with confidence. More importantly, you will understand WHY each piece exists - because in secure software development, understanding the 'why' is just as important as knowing the 'how'.
theory.why_important: Java powers over 3 billion devices worldwide. It runs banking systems that handle your money, hospital systems that store your medical records, government services that hold your personal data, and the Android phones in your pocket. When a Java programmer writes code with structural errors or poor output handling, it can create security vulnerabilities that affect millions of people. For example, printing sensitive data to the wrong output stream has caused real data breaches. Learning to write correct, well-structured Java from day one means learning to protect people. That is not just coding - that is responsibility.

key_concepts.concept: The Java Program Structure - Your Recipe Template
key_concepts.explanation: Every Java program follows a strict template, just like every letter needs an address, greeting, body, and signature. The template has three layers: (1) The CLASS - this is the container, like a recipe card. Its name MUST match the filename. (2) The MAIN METHOD - this is the starting point. Java always looks for 'public static void main(String[] args)' to know where to begin. Think of it like the 'Play' button on a music player. (3) Your CODE STATEMENTS - these are the actual instructions inside the main method. Each statement ends with a semicolon (;), just like each sentence in English ends with a full stop.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Think of the curly braces { } as walls of a room. The class is the building, the main method is a room inside it, and your code lives inside that room. Every opening brace { needs a closing brace }.

key_concepts.explanation: System.out.println() is how your program talks to the outside world. Break the name down: 'System' is the computer itself, 'out' is the output channel (like a speaker), and 'println' means 'print this line and then move to the next line'. Whatever you put inside the parentheses and double quotes gets displayed on screen. This is called 'standard output' or 'stdout'. Every message, every result, every error your program needs to communicate goes through output methods like this one.
key_concepts.ai_hint: The 'ln' at the end of println stands for 'line' - it adds a new line after printing. Think of it like pressing Enter after typing a sentence.

key_concepts.concept: System.out.print() - Printing WITHOUT a New Line
key_concepts.explanation: System.out.print() works exactly like println() but with one key difference: it does NOT move to the next line afterward. Imagine a typewriter that prints a word but does not push the paper up. The next thing you print appears right next to it on the same line. This is useful when you want to build up a line of text piece by piece, or when you want two outputs to appear side by side.
key_concepts.ai_hint: print() = stay on the same line. println() = move to next line after printing. Think: 'ln' = 'line break'.

key_concepts.concept: System.out.printf() - Formatted Output Like a Template
key_concepts.explanation: System.out.printf() is the most powerful output method. Think of it like a form letter with blanks to fill in. You write a template string with special placeholders: %s for text (strings), %d for whole numbers (integers), %f for decimal numbers (floats), and %n for a new line. Then you provide the values to fill in those blanks. This gives you precise control over how your output looks - you can set column widths, decimal places, and alignment.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Think of printf like a Mad Libs game: you write the sentence with blanks (%s, %d, %f), then provide the words/numbers to fill them in.

key_concepts.explanation: Comments are messages that Java completely ignores. They are written for humans, not for the computer. Think of them like sticky notes attached to your recipe. There are three types: (1) Single-line comments start with // and last until the end of that line. (2) Multi-line comments start with /* and end with */ - everything between is ignored. (3) Javadoc comments start with /** and end with */ - these are special documentation comments that tools can read. Good comments explain WHY you did something, not WHAT you did. The code already shows the 'what'.
key_concepts.ai_hint: A common mistake: over-commenting obvious code. Don't write '// print hello' above System.out.println("hello"). Instead, explain WHY: '// Greet the user when the application starts'.

key_concepts.concept: Escape Characters - Special Symbols in Text
key_concepts.explanation: What if you want to print a double quote inside a string? The computer would get confused because it thinks the quote ends the string. Escape characters solve this problem. The backslash (\) tells Java: 'the next character is special, treat it differently'. Key escape characters: \n = new line (like pressing Enter), \t = tab (like pressing Tab for indentation), \" = a literal double quote inside a string, \\ = a literal backslash, \' = a literal single quote.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Think of \ as a secret signal. It says: 'Hey Java, the next character after me is not what you think - treat it specially!' Without it, Java would misread your string.

key_concepts.explanation: The + operator does double duty in Java. With numbers, it adds them. With strings, it joins (concatenates) them together. When you mix strings and numbers, Java automatically converts the number to text and joins them. Think of it like a paper chain: each + links another piece onto the chain. Be careful though: the order matters! '5 + 3' gives 8 (math), but '"5" + "3"' gives "53" (joining text).
key_concepts.ai_hint: The parentheses trick is crucial: 'text' + 5 + 3 = 'text53' because Java reads left to right, converting everything to text. But 'text' + (5 + 3) = 'text8' because the parentheses force the math to happen first.

key_concepts.concept: The Semicolon Rule - Every Statement Needs a Full Stop
key_concepts.explanation: In English, every sentence ends with a full stop. In Java, every statement ends with a semicolon (;). Forget it, and Java throws an error and refuses to run your program. But not everything needs a semicolon: class declarations, method declarations, and lines with curly braces do NOT end with semicolons. Only executable statements (things that DO something) need them. This is one of the most common beginner mistakes, and even experienced developers sometimes miss one.
key_concepts.example:
// These NEED semicolons:
System.out.println("Hello");   // statement
int x = 5;                      // variable declaration

// These do NOT need semicolons:
public class MyClass {          // class header
public static void main(String[] args) {  // method header
if (x > 0) {                   // control structure
key_concepts.ai_hint: Quick rule: if the line DOES something (prints, assigns, calculates), it needs a semicolon. If the line STARTS a block (class, method, if, loop), it does NOT. The curly brace { takes the place of the semicolon.

key_concepts.concept: Case Sensitivity - Java Cares About Capital Letters
key_concepts.explanation: Java is case-sensitive, meaning it treats uppercase and lowercase letters as completely different. 'System' is NOT the same as 'system' or 'SYSTEM'. 'String' is NOT the same as 'string'. 'main' is NOT the same as 'Main'. This trips up many beginners because most human languages do not work this way. The conventions are: class names start with Uppercase (HelloWorld), method and variable names start with lowercase (main, myName), and constants are ALL_CAPS (MAX_SIZE). These are not just style choices - some are required by Java itself.
key_concepts.ai_hint: When you get an error about something 'not found' or 'cannot resolve symbol', the FIRST thing to check is your capitalisation. It is the number one cause of 'mystery' errors for beginners.

key_concepts.concept: Curly Braces and Indentation - Organising Your Code
key_concepts.explanation: Curly braces { } define blocks of code that belong together. Think of them like the walls of nested boxes: the class is the outer box, the main method is a box inside it, and if/else blocks would be even smaller boxes inside that. Indentation (adding spaces at the start of lines) is not required by Java, but it makes your code readable. The standard is 4 spaces for each nesting level. Professional code ALWAYS uses consistent indentation. In fact, poorly indented code is considered a code smell in security reviews because it makes bugs harder to spot.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Count your braces! Every { must have a matching }. A helpful trick: when you type {, immediately type } and then fill in the code between them. This prevents the most common structural error in Java.

complete_examples.title: Example 1: The Minimal Java Program
complete_examples.description: The absolute simplest valid Java program. Every Java journey starts here.
complete_examples.ai_explanation: This is the skeleton every Java program is built on. The class name 'MinimalProgram' must match the filename (MinimalProgram.java). The main method is the entry point - Java starts running here. The println statement outputs text followed by a new line.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Change the message to include your name. Then add a second println to print your favourite colour on a new line.

complete_examples.title: Example 2: Multiple Print Statements
complete_examples.description: Using multiple println statements to create multi-line output.
complete_examples.output:
====================
  WELCOME TO JAVA  
====================
Your journey starts here.
Let's learn step by step.
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Each println() outputs its text and then moves to a new line. The statements run in order, top to bottom, creating a nice banner effect. The spaces inside the strings create centring.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Create your own ASCII art banner using println statements. Try making a box with your name inside it.

complete_examples.title: Example 3: print() vs println() - Side by Side
complete_examples.description: See the exact difference between print (stays on line) and println (moves to next line).
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Lines 4-6: print() keeps the cursor on the same line, so 'Hello ', 'World ', and '!' all appear together. Line 6 uses println() so it finally moves to the next line. Lines 9-11: each println() outputs its text AND moves to a new line, so each word appears on its own line.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Use only print() statements (not println) to output your full address on one line, with commas between parts. Then use one final println() to end the line.

complete_examples.title: Example 4: Escape Characters in Action
complete_examples.description: Master all escape characters with clear before/after examples.
complete_examples.output:
First line
Second line
Third line
---
Name	Age	City
Alice	25	London
Bob	30	Paris
---
She said "Hello!" to everyone.
File path: C:\Users\Documents
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Each escape sequence starts with a backslash (\). Java sees the backslash and thinks: 'the next character has a special meaning'. \n creates a line break in the output. \t creates a tab space (good for aligning columns). \" lets you include quote marks inside a string. \\ prints a single backslash (because \ alone would start an escape sequence).
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Create a 'receipt' printout using \t for column alignment and \n for line breaks, all inside a single println statement.

complete_examples.title: Example 5: String Concatenation - Joining Text
complete_examples.description: Using the + operator to combine strings, numbers, and variables.
complete_examples.output:
Hello World
The year is 2025
Sum: 53
Sum: 8
Alice is 25 years old and lives in London.
complete_examples.ai_explanation: The + operator joins things together into text. When Java encounters a string + number, it converts the number to text first. The 'order trap' on lines 11-12 is crucial: without parentheses, Java processes left-to-right, turning everything into text. With parentheses, the math happens first.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Create variables for a product name, price (double), and quantity (int). Use concatenation to print a receipt line like: '3x Widget @ 9.99 = 29.97'.

complete_examples.title: Example 6: printf() - Formatted Output
complete_examples.description: Using printf for precise control over how your output looks.
complete_examples.output:
Name: Alice
Age: 25 years
Score: 95.68%

NAME            AGE   CITY      
Alice           25    London    
Bob             30    Paris     
Charlie         22    Berlin
complete_examples.ai_explanation: printf uses format specifiers as placeholders: %s = string, %d = integer, %f = float/double, %n = newline. The number between % and the letter controls width: %-15s means 'left-align in a 15-character wide column'. The .2 in %.2f means 'show 2 decimal places'. %% prints a literal percent sign.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Create a formatted student report card showing Name, Subject, and Grade (as a percentage with 1 decimal place) in neat columns.

complete_examples.title: Example 7: Comments - Three Types Explained
complete_examples.description: How to write useful comments that help other developers (and future you).
complete_examples.output:
Welcome, New Learner!
Ready to learn Java?
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Three comment types: (1) // single-line: quick notes, TODO reminders. (2) /* */ multi-line: longer explanations spanning multiple lines. (3) /** */ Javadoc: special documentation that tools can generate API docs from. Notice the commented-out code on the last line - using // to temporarily disable code is a common debugging technique.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add Javadoc comments to a class that describes a calculator. Include @author and @version tags, and explain what the class does.

complete_examples.title: Example 8: Common Errors and How to Fix Them
complete_examples.description: The top 5 syntax errors every beginner makes - and the fixes.
complete_examples.output:
Error 1 fixed: semicolon added
Error 2 fixed: System with capital S
Error 3 fixed: both quotes present
Error 4 fixed: all braces matched
Error 5 fixed: names match
complete_examples.ai_explanation: These five errors account for about 80% of all beginner compilation failures. The Java compiler catches all of them, but its error messages can be confusing. Learning to recognise these patterns will save you hours of frustration. Tip: always read the error message line number - the actual error is usually on that line or the line above it.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: I will show you a piece of broken code with 3 errors hidden in it. Can you find and fix all three? (Try the exercises tab!)

complete_examples.title: Example 9: Building a User Profile Display
complete_examples.description: Combining everything learned into a practical mini-project.
complete_examples.output:
========================================
           USER PROFILE
========================================
  Name:      Sarah Chen
  Age:       28
  Email:     sarah@example.com
  Rating:    4.9 / 5.0
  Projects:  12 completed
========================================
  Sarah Chen has been a member since 2022.
  "Keep coding, keep growing!" - Sarah Chen
========================================
complete_examples.ai_explanation: This example combines everything: println for simple lines, printf for formatted data (notice %.1f rounds 4.876 to 4.9), string concatenation to build sentences with variables, and escape characters (\n for blank lines, \" for quotes in the motto). This is the kind of structured output you would build in real applications.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Modify this to display YOUR profile. Add two more fields: 'Country' and 'Favourite Language'. Make sure the alignment still looks clean.

complete_examples.title: Example 10: A Simple Invoice Generator
complete_examples.description: Real-world application: building formatted business output.
complete_examples.ai_explanation: This brings together everything: printf for precise column alignment (%-20s = left-aligned 20 chars, %8.2f = 8 chars wide with 2 decimals), string concatenation, variables, and calculations. Notice how the format widths create perfectly aligned columns. This is exactly how real business applications format output.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add a fourth item, apply a 10% discount before tax, and add a 'Discount' line to the invoice. Make sure the columns still align!

complete_examples.title: Example 11: ASCII Art with Print Statements
complete_examples.description: Creative coding: using print and println to create visual art.
complete_examples.ai_explanation: ASCII art is a fun way to practise println() and escape characters. Notice the double backslashes in the roof (\\) because a single backslash would start an escape sequence. The empty println() on line 11 creates a blank line between the house and the cup.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Create your own ASCII art - a tree, a robot, or your initials in block letters!

complete_examples.title: Example 12: System Information Display
complete_examples.description: Using Java's built-in properties to display real system information.
complete_examples.output:
===== SYSTEM INFORMATION =====

Java Version:    17.0.2
Java Vendor:     Oracle Corporation
OS Name:         Linux
OS Version:      5.15.0
OS Architecture: amd64
User Name:       student
User Home:       /home/student
User Directory:  /home/student/projects

==============================

[SECURITY NOTE]
Be careful! Printing system info can
leak sensitive data in production.
Always review what your logs expose.
complete_examples.ai_explanation: System.getProperty() retrieves information about the Java runtime and operating system. This example shows formatted output using printf with fixed-width labels for alignment. The SECURITY NOTE is important: in real applications, accidentally printing system paths, user names, or Java versions can give attackers valuable information for crafting targeted attacks. This is related to CWE-200 (Information Exposure).
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add the current time using System.currentTimeMillis() and format it. Also add the available memory using Runtime.getRuntime().freeMemory().

visual_diagram.title: How a Java Program Runs - Complete Flow
visual_diagram.content: JAVA PROGRAM EXECUTION FLOW How your code goes from text file to running program [START] You write code in a .java file | v [STEP 1] WRITE YOUR CODE +------------------------------------------+ | public class Hello { | | public static void main(...) {| | System.out.println("Hi"); | | } | | } | +------------------------------------------+ Save as: Hello.java [!] Filename MUST match class name! | v [STEP 2] COMPILE javac Hello.java +------------------------------------------+ | The Java Compiler checks your code: | | | | [OK] Spelling correct? YES | | [OK] Semicolons present? YES | | [OK] Braces matched? YES | | [OK] Types correct? YES | +------------------------------------------+ | v Compilation successful! Creates: Hello.class (This is bytecode - the computer-readable version) | v [STEP 3] RUN java Hello +------------------------------------------+ | The JVM (Java Virtual Machine) runs it: | | | | 1. Loads Hello.class | | 2. Finds main() method | | 3. Runs code inside main() | | 4. Outputs: Hi | +------------------------------------------+ | v [OUTPUT] Hi WHAT IF SOMETHING GOES WRONG? [COMPILE ERROR] If your code has syntax mistakes: +------------------------------------------+ | Missing semicolon? STOP - fix it! | | Wrong capitalisation? STOP - fix it! | | Missing brace? STOP - fix it! | | Unmatched quotes? STOP - fix it! | +------------------------------------------+ The compiler tells you the line number! [RUNTIME ERROR] Code compiles but crashes when running: +------------------------------------------+ | Divide by zero? CRASH at runtime | | Null value used? CRASH at runtime | | Array out of bounds? CRASH at runtime | +------------------------------------------+ [REMEMBER] 1. Write code in .java file (class name = filename) 2. Compile with javac (catches syntax errors) 3. Run with java (no .class extension needed) 4. Read error messages - they tell you what and where!

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 1: Write Your First Complete Program
practice_exercises.description: Create a complete Java program that prints 'Hello, Java!' to the screen. You need the class, the main method, and a println statement.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Let's write your very first Java program! Think of it like writing a recipe: you need the recipe card (class), the starting step (main method), and the instruction (println). I have given you the skeleton - just fill in the blanks!

hints.message: The main method signature is: public static void main(String[] args) { }
Inside the main method, use: System.out.println("Hello, Java!");

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 2: Fix the Broken Program
practice_exercises.description: This program has THREE syntax errors. Find and fix all of them so it compiles and runs correctly.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Detective time! This program has 3 bugs hiding in it. Read each line carefully - check the class structure, the method signature, and the print statement. Can you find all three?

hints.message: Error 1: Class names should start with a capital letter. 'greeting' should be 'Greeting'.
Error 2: The method name 'Main' should be 'main' (lowercase m). Java is case-sensitive!
Error 3: Two problems on the println line - 'system' needs a capital 'S', and the line is missing a semicolon at the end.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 3: Predict the Output
practice_exercises.description: Read this code carefully and write down EXACTLY what you think will appear on screen. Then run it to check!
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Reading code is just as important as writing code! Look at each line and imagine you are the computer - what would you display? Write your prediction BEFORE running the code.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Line A
Line B
Line C

hints.message: Each println() prints its text and then moves to a new line.
The statements run in order: first A, then B, then C.

Output:
Line A
Line B
Line C

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 4: Spot the Missing Pieces
practice_exercises.description: This program is incomplete. Add the missing parts to make it compile and print 'Java is fun!'.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: This program has gaps - like a puzzle with missing pieces. Fill in the blanks marked with ??? to complete the program!

hints.message: The first ??? is the method name that Java looks for as a starting point.
The second ??? is the class that gives us access to output. It starts with 'S'.
Replace first ??? with 'main' and second ??? with 'System'.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 5: Build a Program from Scratch
practice_exercises.description: Write a complete Java program called 'AboutMe' that prints your name, your favourite colour, and your favourite food on three separate lines.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Time to build something entirely on your own! Create a class called AboutMe with all three parts: class, main method, and three println statements. You can do this!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
My name is [Your Name]
My favourite colour is [Your Colour]
My favourite food is [Your Food]

hints.message: Start with: public class AboutMe {
Then add the main method inside the class braces.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 6: Match the Error to the Fix
practice_exercises.description: Each line has a DIFFERENT error. Fix each one independently. The program should print three lines when all errors are fixed.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: This is like a spelling test for Java! Each println statement has a unique error. Fix them one by one. Read the error messages the compiler gives you as clues!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Line 1: Fixed!
Line 2: Fixed!
Line 3: Fixed!

hints.message: Line 1 is actually correct! No error there.
Line 2: 'Println' has a capital P. It should be 'println' (lowercase p).
Line 3: The text is missing double quotes around it. Should be: System.out.println("Line 3: Fixed!");

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 7: Reorder the Scrambled Code
practice_exercises.description: These lines of code are in the WRONG order. Rearrange them to create a valid Java program that prints 'Order restored!'.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: These code lines got scrambled! Think about the correct nesting order: class wraps method, method wraps statements. Put them back in order!

hints.message: The class declaration must come first (Line E).
The main method goes inside the class (Line C). The println goes inside the method (Line B).

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 8: What is Wrong? Read the Error Message
practice_exercises.description: Run this code, read the error message, and fix the problem. Learning to read error messages is a superpower!
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Real developers read error messages constantly. Run this broken code, look at what Java tells you, and use that information to fix it. The error message IS the clue!

hints.message: Look at the error message - it will mention a specific line number. Focus there.
Line 4 is missing a semicolon at the end of the line.
Add a semicolon at the end of line 4: System.out.println("Calculating result...");

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 9: Class Name vs Filename Challenge
practice_exercises.description: This program compiles but has a bad practice. The class is named 'x'. Rename it to something meaningful and add appropriate comments.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Working code is not enough - code should also be READABLE. This program works but uses terrible names. Refactor it to be professional and meaningful!

hints.message: Class names should describe what the program does. 'WeatherReport' would be much better than 'x'.
Add a Javadoc comment above the class and a single-line comment explaining each section.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 10: Multiple Classes Concept
practice_exercises.description: Write TWO separate programs: one called 'Morning' that prints a morning greeting, and one called 'Evening' that prints an evening greeting.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: In real projects, you will work with many files. Practise creating two separate complete programs. Each needs its own class name, main method, and output!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Program 1: Good morning! Time to code!
Program 2: Good evening! Great work today!

hints.message: Each program needs: public class [Name] { main method { println } }
Remember: each class would normally be in its own .java file.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 11: Print a Menu
practice_exercises.description: Create a restaurant menu display using println. Include a title, at least 5 items with prices, and a footer.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Imagine you are building the display screen for a restaurant. Use println to create a menu that looks professional and organised!

hints.message: Use dots (..) between the item name and price to create a visual connection.
Use = or - characters for the header and footer borders.
Each line is a separate System.out.println() statement. Count your spaces to align the prices!

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 12: Building Output with print()
practice_exercises.description: Use ONLY System.out.print() (not println) to build the sentence 'I love Java programming!' piece by piece, then end the line.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Challenge: build a sentence word by word using only print(), then use one println() to finish the line. This shows you exactly how print() keeps everything on one line!

hints.message: System.out.print("I "); prints 'I ' and stays on the same line.
Follow with System.out.print("love "); then more print() calls...

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 13: Countdown with print()
practice_exercises.description: Use print() to display a countdown on a single line: 5... 4... 3... 2... 1... LAUNCH!
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Create a rocket countdown all on one line! Each number should be followed by '... ' and the final word should be 'LAUNCH!' Use print() for the numbers and println() for the last word!

hints.message: Start with: System.out.print("5... ");
Continue with more print() calls for each number.
End with: System.out.println("LAUNCH!");

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 14: printf() - Format a Student Record
practice_exercises.description: Use printf() to display a student record with perfectly aligned labels and values.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Time to learn printf! It is like a form with blanks - you write the template with %s, %d, and %f placeholders, then fill in the values. Let's format a student record!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Name:      Alice Johnson
ID:        20251234
GPA:       3.87
Major:     Computer Science

hints.message: printf syntax: System.out.printf("Label: %s%n", variable);
%s = string, %d = integer, %f = float/double, %n = new line

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 15: printf() - Column Alignment
practice_exercises.description: Use printf() with width specifiers to create a perfectly aligned table of countries and their capitals.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Professional tables need perfect alignment! printf lets you set column widths. %-20s means 'left-align in 20 characters'. Let's build a beautiful table!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
COUNTRY           CAPITAL          POPULATION
-------------------------------------------------
United Kingdom    London           67,886,011
France            Paris            65,273,511
Germany           Berlin           83,783,942
Italy             Rome             60,461,826

hints.message: Start with the header: System.out.printf("%-18s %-16s %12s%n", "COUNTRY", "CAPITAL", "POPULATION");
Use %,d to format numbers with commas: System.out.printf("%-18s %-16s %,12d%n", "UK", "London", 67886011);
The %-18s means: left-align (-), 18 characters wide, string type (s). The %,12d means: comma-separated, 12 characters wide, integer type (d).

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 16: Mixing print, println, and printf
practice_exercises.description: Use ALL THREE output methods in one program to create a progress bar display.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Let's use all three output methods together! We will build a fake progress bar - print() for the bar itself, printf() for the percentage, and println() for labels.

hints.message: Use System.out.print("["); to start the bar, then print # and . characters.
Use System.out.printf("] %.1f%%%n", percentage); to show the percentage. Remember %% prints a literal %.
Build each bar line by line: print("["), print("##########.........."), printf("] %.1f%%%n", 50.0);

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 17: printf() - Receipt with Calculations
practice_exercises.description: Create a formatted receipt using printf. Show items, quantities, prices, subtotal, tax, and total.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Real-world challenge! Build a shop receipt with proper number formatting. This combines printf column alignment with arithmetic. Take your time!

hints.message: Calculate each line total: double total1 = price1 * qty1;
Use printf with alignment: System.out.printf("%-14s %3d %8.2f %10.2f%n", item1, qty1, price1, total1);
For tax: double subtotal = total1 + total2 + total3; double tax = subtotal * 0.20; double grand = subtotal + tax;

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 18: printf() Format Specifier Quiz
practice_exercises.description: Predict what each printf statement will output. Write your predictions, then run the code to check.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Test your understanding of format specifiers! For each printf, predict the exact output BEFORE running the code. This builds your mental model of how formatting works.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Hi|
Hi        |
3.142
00042
Alice scored 85 out of 100 (85.0%)

hints.message: %10s = right-align in 10 characters. %-10s = left-align in 10 characters.
%.3f = show 3 decimal places. %05d = pad with zeros to 5 digits.
The | character helps you see the alignment. Spaces fill the remaining width.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 19: Print Number Patterns
practice_exercises.description: Use println or printf to create a triangle number pattern.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Create a number pattern using only print statements (no loops yet!). This builds your understanding of output positioning and teaches you to think about patterns!

hints.message: For now, just use 5 separate println statements - one for each row.

System.out.println("1");
System.out.println("1 2"); ... and so on.
Later when you learn loops, you can generate this automatically! For now, hardcoding each line is fine.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 20: Print vs Println Challenge
practice_exercises.description: Using ONLY the provided print() and println() calls (no editing them), rearrange them to produce the exact target output.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Puzzle time! You have a set of print and println statements. Rearrange them (do not change the text inside) to produce the target output. Think about which ones add newlines!

hints.message: Start with the pieces that build 'Hello World!' on the first line.
print("Hello ") + print("World") + println("!") = first line with newline.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 21: printf() - Temperature Converter Display
practice_exercises.description: Display a temperature conversion table using printf for perfect alignment.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Let's build something practical! Create a temperature conversion table showing Celsius and Fahrenheit side by side. Use printf for clean column formatting.

hints.message: For the data rows use: System.out.printf("%8.1f   |   %8.1f%n", celsius, fahrenheit);
Calculate Fahrenheit: double f = celsius * 9.0 / 5.0 + 32.0;
Print the header with println, then use printf for each data row.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 22: Creative ASCII Banner
practice_exercises.description: Create a visually impressive ASCII art banner that displays your name or a word using block letters.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Let's get creative! Build a large ASCII art banner. This is fun AND it practises precise output placement. Each character in your name becomes a block of text!

hints.message: Each line of the banner is one println() statement.
Use spaces carefully to align the letters. Graph paper or a text editor helps!
Start simple: try block letters made of * characters. Each letter is about 5 characters wide and 5 lines tall.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 23: Add Meaningful Comments
practice_exercises.description: This code works but has ZERO comments. Add appropriate comments to make it understandable for someone reading it for the first time.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Code without comments is like a recipe without any explanation - technically complete but hard to follow. Add comments that explain the WHY, not just the WHAT!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Previous balance: $1500.00
Deposit:          $250.00
Withdrawal:       $89.99
New balance:      $1660.01

hints.message: Add a Javadoc comment above the class explaining what it does.
Add comments explaining the section of variable declarations and the calculation logic.

Good comment: // Calculate new balance after today's transactions
Bad comment: // Add deposit and subtract withdrawal (this just repeats the code!)

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 24: Comment Types Practice
practice_exercises.description: Write a program that demonstrates all three comment types: single-line, multi-line, and Javadoc.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Practise all three types of Java comments in one program. Include at least one of each type, and make them meaningful - not just filler text!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Comment types: //, /* */, /** */
Good comments explain WHY, not WHAT
Comments are ignored by the compiler

hints.message: Javadoc format: /** description @author @version */
Multi-line format: /* line 1\n * line 2\n * line 3 */
Remember: comments don't change the output - they only help humans reading the code.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 25: Fix Bad Comments
practice_exercises.description: This code has TERRIBLE comments that either state the obvious or are misleading. Replace them with useful comments.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Bad comments are worse than no comments because they mislead! Replace these useless comments with ones that actually add value.

hints.message: Instead of '// declare a variable', explain WHAT it represents: // Base price before tax
Instead of '// multiply by 0.2', explain WHY: // Apply UK VAT rate of 20%
Good comments answer: What is this for? Why this value? What might change later?

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 26: Using Comments for Debugging
practice_exercises.description: This program has a bug. Use comments to temporarily disable lines and isolate the problem.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Professional technique: commenting out code to find bugs! This program crashes. Comment out lines one at a time to find which one causes the error.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Step 1: Starting...
Step 2: Loading data...
Step 3: Processing...done
Step 4: Saving results...
Step 5: Complete!

hints.message: Comment out Step 3 using // and run the program. Does it work now? That means the bug is on that line!
The bug is division by zero: 10 / 0 causes ArithmeticException.
Fix: replace '10 / 0' with a valid expression like '"done"': System.out.println("Step 3: Processing...done");

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 27: Javadoc Documentation
practice_exercises.description: Write a complete Javadoc-documented program with @author, @version, and @param tags.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Javadoc comments are SPECIAL - tools can turn them into beautiful HTML documentation! Learn to write them properly. They start with /** and can include special tags like @author.

hints.message:
Class Javadoc: /**
 * Calculator - Performs basic arithmetic.
 * @author YourName
 * @version 1.0
 */

Method Javadoc: /**
 * Main method - entry point.
 * @param args command line arguments (not used)
 */
Javadoc appears above the element it documents. The first sentence becomes the summary.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 28: TODO Comments in Practice
practice_exercises.description: This program uses TODO comments to mark incomplete sections. Complete all the TODOs.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: TODO comments are a professional convention for marking work that needs to be done. Many IDEs highlight them automatically! Find and complete all the TODOs.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Student: Alex
Test 1: 85
Test 2: 92
Test 3: 78
Average: 85.0

hints.message: Start by declaring: String name = "Alex"; int test1 = 85; int test2 = 92; int test3 = 78;
Calculate average: double average = (test1 + test2 + test3) / 3.0; (use 3.0 not 3 for decimal result!)
Use printf for the average: System.out.printf("Average: %.1f%n", average);

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 29: Newline Escape Character
practice_exercises.description: Use \n to print three lines of an address using a SINGLE println statement.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Challenge: print multiple lines using only ONE println statement! The trick is the \n escape character, which creates a new line inside your string.

hints.message:
Use 
 between each line: "Line1
Line2
Line3"
System.out.println("42 Code Street\nLondon\nEC1A 1BB");
The \n inside the string tells Java to start a new line at that point, even though it is all in one println.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 30: Tab Escape Character Table
practice_exercises.description: Use \t to create a perfectly aligned table of data.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: The \t escape creates a tab stop - perfect for aligning columns! Build a data table using tabs between each column.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Name	Age	City
Alice	25	London
Bob	30	Paris
Charlie	22	Berlin

hints.message: System.out.println("Name\tAge\tCity"); creates the header row.
Each \t inserts a tab character, which typically aligns to the next 8-character boundary.
Note: tabs may not align perfectly if text lengths vary widely. printf with fixed widths is better for precise alignment.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 31: Printing Quotes Inside Strings
practice_exercises.description: Print sentences that contain double quote marks using the \" escape.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: How do you put a " inside a string when " also marks the end of the string? The escape character \" tells Java: 'this is a literal quote, not the end of my string!'
practice_exercises.expected_output:
He said "Hello" to everyone.
The file is called "data.txt".
Java calls it a "String" type.

hints.message: Use \" for each quote character inside the string.
System.out.println("He said \"Hello\" to everyone.");
Every time you need a literal " inside a string, put a \ before it.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 32: Backslash Escape - File Paths
practice_exercises.description: Print Windows file paths correctly using \\.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Windows uses backslashes in file paths, but backslash is also Java's escape character! You need \\ (double backslash) to print a single backslash.

hints.message: Each single \ in the output requires \\ in your code.
System.out.println("C:\\Users\\Student\\Documents");
This is why many programmers prefer forward slashes (/) - they work on all operating systems and don't need escaping!

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 33: Escape Character Mix Challenge
practice_exercises.description: Combine \n, \t, \", and \\ in one program to create a formatted dialogue.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Time to mix ALL the escape characters! Create a formatted dialogue that uses newlines, tabs, quotes, and backslashes.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Teacher: "What is the escape character in Java?"
	Student: "It's the backslash: \"
Teacher: "Correct! And \n means?"
	Student: "New line!"

hints.message: For Student indentation, start the string with \t
For the backslash in 'It's the backslash: \', use: \\\"  (two backslashes then escaped quote)
To print the literal text \n, use \\n (escaped backslash followed by n).

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 34: Escape Characters - Predict the Output
practice_exercises.description: Read each println and predict the EXACT output before running.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Mental exercise! For each line, figure out what the escape characters will produce. Write your prediction, then run to verify. This builds deep understanding!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
A	B	C
Line1
Line2
Path: C:\temp
Say "Hi"
Mix:	"test"
\done\

hints.message: \t = tab space, \n = new line, \" = literal quote, \\ = literal backslash.
The last line is the trickiest: \t creates tab, \"test\" puts test in quotes, \n creates newline, \\done\\ puts backslashes around 'done'.
Process left to right: each \ followed by a character = one output character.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 35: Create a JSON-Like String
practice_exercises.description: Print a JSON-formatted string using escape characters for the quotes and structure.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Real-world skill! JSON uses lots of double quotes and structure. Print a JSON object using escape characters. This is something you will do often in web development!

hints.message: Use \n for newlines and \" for each quote mark inside the string.
You could use one long println: System.out.println("{\n  \"name\": \"Alice\",\n  ...");
Or use multiple println statements - one per line. Either approach works!

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 36: Box Drawing with Escape Characters
practice_exercises.description: Create a boxed message using escape characters to control the layout.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Use escape characters to draw a box around a message. This combines \n for new lines with careful spacing!

hints.message: Build the string with \n between each line of the box.
Count your characters! Each line inside the box should be exactly the same width for the box to look right.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 37: Carriage Return \r Experiment
practice_exercises.description: Experiment with the \r (carriage return) escape character to see how it differs from \n.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: The \r escape character moves the cursor back to the beginning of the SAME line (without going to a new line). This can create interesting overwrite effects!

hints.message: \r moves the cursor to the START of the current line without going to a new line.
So 'Before\rAfter' prints 'Before', goes back to start, then prints 'After' OVER the top of 'Before'.
The result depends on your system. On most systems: 'After' overwrites 'Befor', leaving 'Aftere' or similar. Fun to experiment with!

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 38: Escape Character Reference Card
practice_exercises.description: Create a printed reference card that demonstrates EVERY common escape character.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Build a cheat sheet IN CODE! Print a reference card that shows each escape character, what it does, and an example. This is great for your own reference later!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
========================================
   JAVA ESCAPE CHARACTER REFERENCE
========================================
\n  = New line       Demo: Line1
Line2
\t  = Tab           Demo: Col1	Col2
\"  = Double quote  Demo: She said "Hi"
\\  = Backslash     Demo: C:\path
\'  = Single quote  Demo: It's done
\r  = Carriage ret  Demo: (returns to line start)
========================================

hints.message: For the \n demo, you'll need actual newline in the output: System.out.println("\\n  = New line       Demo: Line1\nLine2");
Notice: to print the literal text \n, you write \\n (escaped backslash + n).
This is meta: you're using escape characters to print documentation ABOUT escape characters!

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 39: Basic String Concatenation
practice_exercises.description: Join two strings together using the + operator.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: The + operator joins strings together like a chain. Let's start simple!

hints.message: Use + to join: firstName + " " + lastName
Don't forget the space " " between them!
System.out.println(firstName + " " + lastName);

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 40: Concatenation with Numbers
practice_exercises.description: Mix strings and numbers in concatenation to create informational sentences.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: When you use + with a string and a number, Java automatically converts the number to text. But watch out for the order - it matters!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Product: Laptop
Price: 999 pounds
Quantity: 3
Total: 2997 pounds

hints.message: System.out.println("Product: " + product);
For the total, use parentheses to force math: (price * quantity)
System.out.println("Total: " + (price * quantity) + " pounds");

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 41: The Concatenation Order Trap
practice_exercises.description: Predict the output of tricky concatenation expressions where order matters.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: This is where concatenation gets tricky! When does + mean 'add numbers' and when does it mean 'join text'? The answer depends on the ORDER. Predict each output!

hints.message: Java processes + from LEFT to RIGHT. Once it encounters a String, everything after becomes concatenation.
Line 3: 5 + 3 = 8 (math), then 8 + " is the answer" = "8 is the answer" (concat).

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 42: Build a Personal Profile with Concatenation
practice_exercises.description: Create a detailed profile display using variables and concatenation.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Build a personal profile by declaring variables and joining them with descriptive text. This is how real user interfaces display information!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Hi, I'm Alex, a 25-year-old from London.
I love coding and have 3 years of experience.
Fun fact: In 75 years, I'll be 100!

hints.message: String name = "Alex"; int age = 25; String city = "London";
"Hi, I'm " + name + ", a " + age + "-year-old from " + city + "."
For the calculation: (100 - age) inside the concatenation. Use parentheses!

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 43: Multi-Variable Concatenation
practice_exercises.description: Combine 6 or more variables into a single formatted output statement.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Long concatenation chains are common in real code. Build a complex sentence using many variables joined together!
practice_exercises.expected_output: On Monday, January 15, 2025, it was sunny and 18 degrees.

hints.message: Build it piece by piece: "On " + day + ", " + month + " " + ...
Don't forget spaces and punctuation in the literal strings!
"On " + day + ", " + month + " " + date + ", " + year + ", it was " + weather + " and " + temp + " degrees."

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 44: Concatenation vs printf Comparison
practice_exercises.description: Print the same data TWO ways: once with concatenation and once with printf. Compare the readability.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Same output, two approaches! Compare concatenation vs printf for the same task. Which do YOU find more readable? Both are valid - know both!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Alice scored 95 points (95.5%)
--- same output, different method ---
Alice scored 95 points (95.5%)

hints.message: Concatenation: name + " scored " + score + " points (" + percentage + "%)"
printf: System.out.printf("%s scored %d points (%.1f%%)%n", name, score, percentage);
Notice: printf needs %% to print a literal %. Concatenation just uses "%".

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 45: Building HTML with Concatenation
practice_exercises.description: Use string concatenation to build a simple HTML snippet (displayed as text).
practice_exercises.ai_intro: In web development, you often build HTML strings in Java! Use concatenation (and escape characters for quotes) to build a simple HTML snippet.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Welcome

This is my first web page.

hints.message: You can use \n and concatenation: "<html>\n" + "  <head>..."
Or use multiple println statements - one per line.

Include the variables: "

" + heading + "

"

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 46: Concatenation - Build an Email Template
practice_exercises.description: Use variables and concatenation to build a personalised email message.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Email templates are a perfect example of concatenation in the real world! Build a personalised email using variables for the recipient name, subject, and key details.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Subject: Your Order ORD-12345 Confirmation

Dear Sarah,

Thank you for your order! Here are the details:
  Order ID: ORD-12345
  Product: Wireless Headphones
  Amount: $79.99
  Estimated Delivery: February 25, 2025

If you have questions, reply to this email.

Best regards,
Java Shop Team

hints.message: Use println for each line, inserting variables with +
For blank lines between paragraphs, use System.out.println();
For the amount, you can concatenate directly: "  Amount: $" + amount - Java converts the double to text.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 47: String Concatenation Security Warning
practice_exercises.description: Understand why concatenating user input into strings can be DANGEROUS from a security perspective.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: SECURITY ALERT! Blindly concatenating user input into strings is one of the top causes of security vulnerabilities (SQL Injection, XSS). This exercise shows WHY it matters.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
SAFE: Hello, Alice! Welcome.
DANGEROUS: SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = "Alice"; DROP TABLE users; --"

[SECURITY LESSON]
Never concatenate user input directly into SQL or HTML.
Attackers can inject malicious code through the input.
Always use prepared statements for SQL and proper escaping for HTML.

hints.message: The malicious input breaks out of the string and adds its own SQL command!
This is called SQL Injection - one of the OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities.
The fix: never build SQL with string concatenation. Use PreparedStatement with ? placeholders instead.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 48: StringBuilder vs Concatenation
practice_exercises.description: Learn when concatenation is fine and when StringBuilder is better (preview of upcoming topics).
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Quick preview of an optimisation concept! Simple concatenation creates many temporary String objects. StringBuilder is more efficient for building long strings. See the difference!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Concatenation: Hello World from Java!
StringBuilder: Hello World from Java!
apple, banana, cherry, date, elderberry

hints.message: StringBuilder sb2 = new StringBuilder(); then sb2.append() for each fruit.
Add commas between fruits: sb2.append("apple"); sb2.append(", banana"); ...
System.out.println(sb2.toString()); to print the final result.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 49: Program Skeleton Builder
practice_exercises.description: Write a complete Java program skeleton from memory in under 30 seconds. Speed drill!
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Speed round! Write a complete Java program skeleton (class + main method + one println) as fast as you can. This should become muscle memory!

hints.message: public class SpeedTest {
public static void main(String[] args) {

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 50: Debug Detective - 5 Errors
practice_exercises.description: This program has exactly 5 errors. Find and fix ALL of them. The output should show a perfect weather report.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Put your detective hat on! This program has 5 bugs. Some are easy, some are sneaky. Find them all!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Weather Report for London
Temperature: 18C
Humidity: 65.5%

hints.message: Error 1: 'Public' should be 'public' (lowercase p).
Error 2: London needs quotes: "London". Error 3: Missing semicolon after 65.5.
Error 4: 'system' needs capital 'S'. Error 5: printf is missing a comma before 'humidity'.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 51: Code Review - Rate This Code
practice_exercises.description: This code WORKS but has poor style. Rewrite it with proper naming, comments, indentation, and formatting.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Working code is not always GOOD code! This program runs perfectly but has terrible style. Refactor it to be professional quality. Think: would you be happy showing this to a boss?
practice_exercises.starter_code: public class x{public static void main(String[] args){double a=100;double b=20;double c=a*b/100;double d=a+c;System.out.println(d);}}

hints.message: First: rename the class to something meaningful like 'TaxCalculator'.
Then: put each statement on its own line with proper indentation (4 spaces per level).
Then: rename variables (a=price, b=taxRate, c=taxAmount, d=total) and add comments.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 52: Build a Multiplication Table
practice_exercises.description: Using only println and printf (no loops), create a multiplication table for the number 7.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Create a clean multiplication table! Use printf for aligned columns. Later you'll learn to generate this with loops, but for now, write each line manually.

hints.message: Use: System.out.printf("  %d x %2d = %3d%n", num, 1, num * 1);
%2d pads the multiplier to 2 digits, %3d pads the result to 3 digits for alignment.
Write 12 printf statements, one for each row. The num variable stays 7.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 53: Build a Dashboard Display
practice_exercises.description: Create a server monitoring dashboard using all output techniques.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Combine everything into a real-world project! Build a server monitoring dashboard that would make a sysadmin proud. Use println, printf, concatenation, and escape characters!

hints.message: Start with the header using println and = characters.
For resource bars, calculate how many # vs . to show (e.g., cpuUsage/5 gives # count in a 20-char bar).
Use printf with %,d for comma-formatted connection count: System.out.printf("  Active Connections: %,d%n", activeConnections);

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 54: Error Message Translator
practice_exercises.description: Given real Java compiler error messages, explain what they mean in plain English and show the fix.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Compiler errors can look scary! Let's translate them into plain language. For each error message, print what it means and how to fix it.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
JAVA ERROR MESSAGE TRANSLATOR
============================

Error: ';' expected
Meaning: You forgot a semicolon at the end of a statement.
Fix: Add ; at the end of the line mentioned in the error.

Error: cannot find symbol
Meaning: Java doesn't recognise a name you used.
Fix: Check spelling and capitalisation. Is the variable declared?

Error: class, interface, or enum expected
Meaning: Code is outside any class. Maybe an extra closing brace.
Fix: Make sure all code is inside a class. Check your braces.

Error: unclosed string literal
Meaning: A string is missing its closing double quote.
Fix: Add the missing " at the end of your string.

Error: illegal start of expression
Meaning: Java found something unexpected in your code.
Fix: Check the line above for missing semicolons or braces.

hints.message: Use println for each section. Blank lines (println()) separate the entries.
These are the 5 most common errors every Java beginner encounters.
You can format these however you like - the key is the explanations are clear and helpful!

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 55: Build a Quiz Results Display
practice_exercises.description: Create a formatted quiz results screen showing questions, answers, and scores.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Build a complete quiz results screen! This combines formatted output, calculations, and visual presentation. Make it look professional!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
================================
  QUIZ RESULTS: Alex
================================
  Q1: What starts a Java program?     [CORRECT]
  Q2: println adds a newline?         [CORRECT]
  Q3: Java is case-insensitive?       [WRONG]
  Q4: // starts a comment?            [CORRECT]
  Q5: Semicolons are optional?        [WRONG]
================================
  Score: 3 / 5 (60.0%)
  Result: PASS
================================

hints.message: For CORRECT/WRONG: use the boolean - a1 ? "[CORRECT]" : "[WRONG]" (this is the ternary operator).
Count correct answers: int score = (a1?1:0) + (a2?1:0) + (a3?1:0) + (a4?1:0) + (a5?1:0);
If you haven't learned ternary yet, just use string variables: String r1 = "[CORRECT]"; and concatenate them.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 56: Code Trace Challenge
practice_exercises.description: Trace through the code line by line and write the EXACT output. No running allowed first!
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Pure mental exercise! Trace through this code in your head. Write down what EACH line outputs. Only run the code AFTER you've written your prediction!

hints.message: print("A") stays on same line, then println("B") adds B and newline -> AB
print("C") + print("D") + println() -> CD with newline
1+2+"H" = 3+"H" = "3H" (math first). "I"+1+2 = "I1"+2 = "I12" (concat left to right).

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 57: Build a Calendar Month
practice_exercises.description: Create a formatted calendar display for January 2025 using printf and println.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Create a calendar! This is a real formatting challenge. Use printf for aligned columns. January 2025 starts on a Wednesday - make sure the dates line up correctly!

hints.message: Use printf("%3d", day) to right-align each number in a 3-character column.
The first row needs padding: the 1st falls on Wednesday, so pad Monday and Tuesday with spaces.
You can use println for each row, or build with printf. Careful with the spacing!

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 58: Security Audit Report
practice_exercises.description: Create a professional security audit report combining all output techniques.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Final project! Build a realistic security audit report. This is the kind of output a real security tool would produce. Combine every technique you've learned!

hints.message: Use printf for aligned data: System.out.printf("  Critical: %3d  !!!%n", critical);
Use println for borders and section headers.
This is a big exercise - build it section by section. Start with the header, then add each section.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 59: Teach Java to a Friend
practice_exercises.description: Write a program that TEACHES Java basics. Print explanations and code examples that a complete beginner could follow.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: The best test of understanding is teaching! Write a program that acts as a tutorial - explaining Java concepts with examples. If you can teach it, you truly understand it!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
====================================
  LEARN JAVA IN 5 MINUTES!
====================================

LESSON 1: What is a Java Program?
---------------------------------
A Java program is like a recipe.
It has a name (class), a start point (main),
and instructions (your code).

Example structure:
  public class MyProgram {
      public static void main(String[] args) {
          // your code here
      }
  }

LESSON 2: Printing Output
---------------------------------
println("Hi") -> prints Hi and moves to next line
print("Hi")   -> prints Hi and stays on same line
printf("%s",x) -> prints with formatting

[Continue with your own lessons!]

hints.message: Think about what confused YOU when you started. Address those points!
Use println for each line. Include code examples as indented text.
The best tutorials use analogies: 'A class is like a recipe card...'

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 60: Capstone - Build Your Java Portfolio Piece
practice_exercises.description: Create a comprehensive program that demonstrates EVERY concept from this topic. This is your portfolio showpiece!
practice_exercises.ai_intro: CAPSTONE PROJECT! Build one impressive program that uses: class structure, println, print, printf, all comment types, escape characters, string concatenation, and formatted output. Make it something you're proud of!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
[Your creative masterpiece goes here!]

This exercise has no fixed expected output.
The goal is to demonstrate all concepts in
one impressive, well-structured program.

Show us what you can build!

hints.message: Pick a theme you're passionate about - it makes the coding more fun!
Start with the structure: header section, data section, display section. Add comments as you go.
Check your work: Does it have Javadoc? println AND printf? Escape characters? Concatenation? Proper indentation? Comments? If yes, you've mastered this topic!
